coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence tuning behavior of carbon quantum dots with gold nanoparticles via novel intercalation effect of aldicarb
coredata.dc:creator: Sajwan, Reena K.
Lakshmi, G.B.V.S.
Solanki, Pratima R.
coredata.dc:description: A simple, sensitive and rapid fluorometric system has been developed for the detection of aldicarb (ALD) based on inner filter effect (IFE) of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) on fluorescence (FL) intensity of carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Addition of CQDs into AuNPs, gets them aggregated due to electrostatic interaction resulting in quenching the FL intensity of CQDs. With addition of ALD into AuNPs, an intercalated layer was formed between them through Au-N and Au-S bond which reduced IFE of AuNPs. Hence, CQDs FL intensity recovered along with ALD concentration varying between 3.8 and 76 µg L−1 with lower detection limit of 3.02 µg L−1. The spiked real samples study in fruits, vegetables and soft drinks revealed that this sensing platform was repeatable and effective for real samples. The validation of proposed method indicates that the ALD sensor is promising and adaptable for everyday on spot environment and food safety monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Gold nanoparticles
Fluorescence
Aldicarb
Inner filter effect
Food samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Experimental procedure for the detection of ALD
Procedure for detection of ALD in real samples
Effect of AuNPs on fluorescence intensity of CQDs
Enhancement of CQDs fluorescence due to the intercalation effect of ALD
Optimization of experimental conditions for detection of ALD.
Determination of ALD in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India
Department of Biotechnology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM images: (A) CQDs and inset is HR-TEM image of CQDs. (B) TEM image of AuNPs and inset is HR-TEM image of AuNPs. (C) TEM image of AuNPs with CQDs and (D) TEM images of AuNPs in presence of ALD and CQDs.

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectra of AuNPs (a) and CQDs (b); fluorescence excitation (c) and emission spectra (d) of CQDs. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs at different excitation wavelengths vary from 300 to 410 nm. (C) Zeta potential of CQDs, AuNPs and CQDs-AuNPs probe. (D) Fluorescence lifetime of CQDs (a), CQDs-AuNPs (b) and CQDs-AuNPs-ALD (c).

Inner Filter effect of AuNPs. (A) The FL intensity of CQDs along with various concentrations of AuNPs varies from 0.0 to 8.1 nm. (B) FL intensity plot of CQDs -AuNPs probe. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs. (D) UV–Vis absorption spectra of AuNPs.

(A) Recovery of FL intensity of CQDs-AuNPs probe with different concentrations of ALD (0.38, 0.95, 1.9, 3.8, 7.6, 15.2, 22.8, 47.5, 76, and 95 µ µg L−1). (B) Recovered FL intensity of CQDs-AuNPs probe as a function of ALD concentrations. Inset is the linear relationship between fluorescence recovery and concentrations of ALD. (C) Selectivity of CQDs-AuNPs probe towards ALD with other interferents. (D) FL intensity of CQDs-AuNPs probe in the presence of spiked samples.
Validation of CQDs-AuNPs probe with LCMS system.
Mechanism for detection of (A) quenching of FL intensity of CQDs-AuNPs probe. (B) recovery of FL intensity of CQDs-AuNPs probe in presence of ALD.
Comparison between various methods used for the ALD determination.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence tuning behavior of carbon quantum dots with gold nanoparticles via novel intercalation effect of aldicarb

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Special Centre for Nanoscience, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi 110067, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Special Centre for Nanoscience, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi 110067, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple, sensitive and rapid fluorometric system has been developed for the detection of aldicarb (ALD) based on inner filter effect (IFE) of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) on fluorescence (FL) intensity of carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Addition of CQDs into AuNPs, gets them aggregated due to electrostatic interaction resulting in quenching the FL intensity of CQDs. With addition of ALD into AuNPs, an intercalated layer was formed between them through Au-N and Au-S bond which reduced IFE of AuNPs. Hence, CQDs FL intensity recovered along with ALD concentration varying between 3.8 and 76 µg L−1 with lower detection limit of 3.02 µg L−1. The spiked real samples study in fruits, vegetables and soft drinks revealed that this sensing platform was repeatable and effective for real samples. The validation of proposed method indicates that the ALD sensor is promising and adaptable for everyday on spot environment and food safety monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Among the various quantum dots (QDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a newly emerging form of carbon nanomaterials attracted marvelous attention due to their excellent tunable fluorescence properties, good hydrophilic nature, low cytotoxicity, favorable biocompatibility and easy synthetic routes (Wang & Hu, 2014). Due to their inherent advantage, CQDs receive significant interest in numerous fields like bio-imaging (Jiang et al., 2014), catalysis (Zhang, Yao, Guan, Lu, & Lin, 2014), chemiluminescence (Amjadi, Manzoori, & Hallaj, 2015) and fluorescence based sensing (Zhang & Chen, 2014). In the last decade, fluorescence based approaches are widely utilized for the detection of different analytes based on electron transfer process (Li et al., 2018), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) (Wu et al., 2017), aggregation (Huang, Gao, Li, & Wu, 2018; Pu, Zhao, Huang, Wu, & Xu, 2013) and inner filter effect (IFE) (Cao & He, 2019; Wang et al., 2016).

IFE is an essential non-radiation energy exchange mode in fluorescence spectrophotometer, that can be monitored through absorption of excitation and/or emission energy by the quencher in the system (Chen, Yu, & Wang, 2018). Formation of the IFE based detection system, should have two materials in which one has to be fluorescent and other should be quencher. For an excellent performance of the IFE based detection approach, the absorption spectrum of quencher should have significant overlapping with the excitation and/or emission spectrum of fluorescent materials (Zhang et al., 2012).

Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have been of great interest because of their high extinction coefficient (108 cm−1 M−1) to extensive absorption spectrum in visible region which is overlapped with the excitation/emission spectrum of CQDs. Thus, the combination of CQDs and AuNPs works as an effective IFE pair. Based on this mechanism some researchers have reported CQDs based IFE system for the detection of numerous analytes such as Cu2+ (Shao et al., 2005), vitamin B12 (Sun, Yuan, Liu, & Shen, 2018) and hemoglobin (Barati, Shamsipur, & Abdollahi, 2015), in which the FL intensity of CQDs was quenched in the existence of these analytes. This CQDs-AuNPs based IFE system was used for the detection of GSH with lower detection limits of 0.3 µM (Cao & He, 2019). Thus, there is a wide scope to work for pesticide detection, including aldicarb (ALD).

ALD is a systemic carbamate insecticide, having broad spectrum, that is used to control insects and mites on a wide variety of crops, such as citrus fruits, potatoes, grains, peanuts, tomatoes, sugar-beet and tobacco (Liu, Suryoprabowo, Zheng, Song, & Kuang, 2017). The World Health Organization has classified ALD as class IA extremely hazardous pesticide that gets absorbed in the lung, mucous membranes and skin. When it is oxidized, it gets converted into ALD sulfoxide and then to the sulfone and becomes slightly less toxic (Moye & Miles, 1988).

The analytical techniques such as gas chromatography (GC) (Song & McNair, 2002), GC –mass spectrometry (GC–MS) (Carabias-Martínez, García-Hermida, Rodríguez-Gonzalo, & Ruano-Miguel, 2005), high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Damasceno, Adorno, Hirasawa, Varesche, & Zaiat, 2008), HPLC-liquid chromatography (LC)-positive ion atmospheric pressure chemical ionization (APCI)-mass spectrometry (MS) (Nunes, Alonso, Ribeiro, & Barceló, 2000) and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISA) (Rodolico, Giovinazzo, & Mosconi, 1997) are used for the detection of ALD in agricultural products, waters and soils. Although these methods are highly sensitive, they are not as adaptable as this reported method for every day and on the spot environment and food safety monitoring.

Most of the researchers have developed nanomaterials based enzymatic biosensors using different detection mechanisms like colorimetric (Chow, Ho, & Gong, 2015), lateral flow assays (Liu et al., 2017) and fluorometric assays (Azab et al., 2012; Wang, Hou, Dong, Liu, & Li, 2016) for the detection of ALD. But most of these assays require biomolecules including enzymes and aptamers which are costly and have a stability issue at room temperature. Mostly acetyl cholinesterase (AchE) enzyme is being used for detection of ALD and similar other pesticides (Wang et al., 2016; Wu et al., 2017). There is no any other specific enzyme available for the selective and specific detection of ALD. Due to the polyclonal activity of AchE, it is difficult to develop a specific sensor for a particular pesticide detection. Among various analytical measuring methods, fluorescence nanomaterials based detection system has numerous advantages such as rapid detection, simplicity, high sensitivity and easy operation.

In this paper, a novel selective and sensitive, fluorometric assay has been proposed for the detection of ALD in food samples through the IFE of AuNPs on the FL intensity of the CQDs, which is conceptually diverse from the earlier reported CQDs-AuNPs based FRET systems (Dai et al., 2014; Wu et al., 2017). In this IFE mechanism, CQDs-AuNPs probe were used, which offers more flexibility and simplicity of the developed system. Here, CQDs surface was rich with the amine group (–NH2) which shows elevated binding affinity onto the surface of AuNPs (Kumar, Seth, & Kumar, 2014). Upon addition of CQDs into AuNPs, amine groups of CQDs get to interact with AuNPs through the ligand replacement with negatively charged citrate ions, which induced the aggregation of AuNPs leading into quenching the FL intensity of CQDs. It was reported that aggregation of AuNPs does not require any link between quencher and fluorophore or surface modification fluorescent nanomaterials (Cao & He, 2019). But their physical and chemical interaction provides noticeable flexibility and simplicity to develop such type of systems. However, with the addition of ALD, AuNPs would prefer binding with ALD, which resists the aggregation, thus resume the initial wine-red color of AuNPs as shown in Scheme 1 . As a result, IFE of AuNPs diminished and recovered the quenched fluorescent intensity of the CQDs in the presence of ALD. The fruits and vegetables spiked sample study revealed that the developed system was effective for ALD detection in real sample analysis. Further, the validation of the developed system was done with the existing analytical LC-MS technique and found that the proposed system was more sensitive for ALD and has potential use in environmental monitoring and food safety application.

Gold (III) chloride hydrate (HAuCl4), citric acid (CA) (C6H8O7), l-cysteine (C3H7NO2S), ALD (C7H14N2O2S), monocrotophos (C7H14NO5P), methyl-parathion (C10H14NO5PS) and chlorpyrifos (C9H11Cl3NO3PS) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Ethylenediamine (EDA) (C2H8N2,) was purchased from Alfa Assar. Hydrochloric acid (HCl) and potassium chloride (KCl) were obtained from Thermo Fisher Scientific Pvt. Ltd. Trisodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) from SRL; glucose (C6H12O6) and sodium chloride (NaCl) from SD Fine-Chem Limited (SDFCL); ferric chloride (FeCl3) from Hi-media Laboratory Pvt. Ltd; and calcium chloride (CaCl3) from RankemAvantor India Limited were procured. All the materials were used as it is for the solutions preparations with distilled (DI) water.

Surface morphology and size of the synthesized CQDs and AuNPs were characterized by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, JEOL JEM-2200FS, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was performed using spectrum Two, PerkinElmer. A surface charge of CQDs and AuNPs were analyzed with zeta potential (ZEECOM, ZC-3000, Microtech., Japan). UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded using T90 + UV/VIS Spectrometer and Fluorescence spectra were obtained with a Cary Eclipse Fluorescence spectrometer. The solution for the fluorescence measurement was taken in a 1 cm fluorescence cuvette with excitation and emission slit width of 5 nm at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The fluorescence lifetime measurements of CQDs in the presence and absence of AuNPs and ALD were carried out with the help of commercial time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) setup FL-920 (Edinburgh Instruments). Liquid Chromatography Mass Spectroscopy (LC-MS) (UPLC H Class, WATERS, USA) were used for the validation of the developed sensing system.

CQDs having blue fluorescence emission were synthesized according to previously reported procedure by Zhai et al. (2012). During the preparation of CQDs, 1 gm of CA was taken in 10 mL of DI water under continuous stirring to form a transparent solution and then added 384 µL EDA to escalate the carbonization. The colorless solution was then kept in a domestic microwave at 700 W for 3 min, the solution then turned into a red brown clustered signifying the formation of CQDs. Prepared CQDs then allowed to cool down to room temperature (25 °C) and extracted in 10 mL DI water, followed by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 30 min. The CQDs were collected from the supernatant part and stored at 4 °C for further characterization and use.

Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) were prepared using trisodium citrate as a reducing agent as well as capping agent for HAuCl4 (Wu et al., 2017). Typically, 1 mM HAuCl4 solution in 100 mL of DI water was prepared and heated to boiling under the energetic stirring, after that 10 mL of 38.8 mM trisodium citrate solution was quickly injected. After addition of reducing agent color of the solution was altered from yellow to wine red, the reaction was continued for another 30 min under similar condition. This prepared AuNPs solution was cooled down at room temperature (25 °C) and then stored at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental procedure for the detection of ALD
ce:section.ce:para: For the determination of ALD, various concentrations (0.0, 0.38, 0.95, 1.9, 3.8, 7.6, 15.2, 22.8, 47.5, 76, 95 µg L−1) of ALD were prepared. The CQDs were diluted by 200 fold, freshly in DI water and used the same throughout the experiments. For the determination of ALD, 200 µL of different concentrations of ALD and 1 mL AuNPs (4.24 nM) were mixed under gentle shaking in a series of 2 mL micro-centrifuge tube and incubate for 15 min. After that 40 µL of CQDs and DI were added to the reaction solution to maintain the maximum volume of the tube and incubated further for 45 min. The FL intensity of CQDs-AuNPs probe was recorded at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The experimental procedure was kept same throughout all the measurements.

Different interferents were tested to find the applicability of the sensor in real food samples. For that, the common interferents in different food products were selected as follows: Glucose, CA, KCl, NaCl, FeCl3, CaCl3, cystine (0.5 mM concentration of each) and pesticides like monocrotophos, methyl-parathion and chlorpyrifos (0.5 µM each). All the solutions were prepared in DI water and stored at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for detection of ALD in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Further, to check the applicability in real samples, spiked samples of different vegetables (cucumber, tomato and potato), fruits (oranges, grapes and pomegranate) and soft drinks like Pepsi, Coca-Cola and Thumps-Up were purchased from the local vendors.

For vegetables and fruits, the sample extraction was done by grinding the vegetables and fruits using mortar-pestle and then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min and collected the supernatant. The supernatant of these samples was tested to find out the presence of ALD with developed system. Then the juices were spiked with 200 nM (38 µg L−1) ALD concentrations after the extraction and tested for ALD traces.

Direct exposure of ALD to vegetables, cucumber and tomato were done by injecting 2 mL of ALD into the cucumber and tomato and kept for 2 days. Later the juice was extracted according to above mentioned method and tested for the presence of ALD by following the same procedure. Soft drinks were tested and then spiked with 200 nM (38 µg L−1) of ALD concentrations and analyzed for the presence of ALD with developed system

The morphology of the synthesized CQDs and AuNPs were studied using TEM. TEM and HR-TEM images of CQDs and AuNPs are shown in Fig. 1 . TEM image (Fig. 1A) shows that the CQDs have the semi-spherical morphology with a homogeneous distribution of CQDs without any aggregation. The particle size of CQDs lie between 1.5 and 4.2 nm and the average size of the CQDs found to be 2.8 ± 0.5 nm. The HR-TEM image of CQDs shows that most of the CQDs were amorphous in nature and only a few possess crystallinity with the interplanar distance of 2.1 Å in the inset of the Fig. 1A, which depicted the graphitic nature of CQDs (Sarkar, Bohidar, & Solanki, 2018). TEM images of AuNPs show that the shape of the AuNPs is spherical, regular and monodispersed with average particle size of 17.7 ± 2.5 nm as shown in Fig. 1B. The HR-TEM image of AuNPs indicates that the AuNPs were highly crystalline with interplanar distance 2.4 Å. corresponding to Au (111) plane (Biao et al., 2018) as shown in the inset of Fig. 1B. As CQDs added to AuNPs, positively charged amine rich CQDs get to interact with negatively charged citrate ions of AuNPs. Hence, AuNPs get aggregated and forms a cluster with an average diameter about 46 nm showed in TEM image in Fig. 1C. The anti-aggregation phenomenon of AuNPs was again tested by TEM analysis (Fig. 1D) noticeably exhibited that ALD was significantly able to avoid the aggregation of AuNPs in presence of CQDs and hence retained their size and uniformity.

The absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs and AuNPs were recorded using UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectrometer, respectively. Fig. 2 (A) shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of CQDs indicating two prominent peaks around 236 nm and 350 nm. Peak at 236 nm and 350 nm communicate to π-π* transition of aromatic CC bond, and n-π* transition of the CO bond, respectively. The n-π* transition have a prominent impact on the FL intensity spectra of CQDs (Emam, Loutfy, Mostafa, Awad, & Mohamed, 2017; Sri, Kumar, Panda, & Solanki, 2018). Fluorescence emission shows the strong blue emission at 458 nm recorded when CQDs excited at the wavelength of 350 nm. Fig. 2(A) inset shows the emission of CQDs upon the exposure of the UV-lamp. Absorption spectra of AuNPs clearly show a strong SPR peak at 524 nm, which is significantly overlapped with fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs; results indicated the satisfactory condition for the construction of the IFE based system.

Fig. 2(B) shows a strong and bright blue fluorescence emission at 458 nm observed with different excitation wavelengths vary from 300 to 410 nm. It was observed that with the variation in the excitation wavelength (300–410 nm), the fluorescence emission peak position around 458 nm remains unchanged. The excitation independent FL intensity behavior may be ascribed to the surface morphology and uniformity in size that reduced the surface defects of CQDs (Sri et al., 2018). The size uniformity was also confirmed by the TEM observation.

The functional groups present on the surface of CQDs and AuNPs were studied using FTIR spectrum (Supplementary data; Fig. S1 (B)). FTIR spectrum of CQDs exhibit a broad absorption peak around 2974 cm−1 due to CH asymmetric stretching vibration. Vibrational IR absorption peak around 1712 cm−1 represents the CO stretching, while bending vibration of CN shows a sharp peak around 1564 cm−1, representing that there are plenty of amino groups on the surface of CQDs (Zhai et al., 2012). The availability of amino groups on the surface of CQDs confirmed with positive zeta potential (ζ = +3.88 mV; Fig. 2(C)). The absorption peak near 3500 cm−1 and 1402 cm−1 corresponds to OH bending vibration and C-OH stretching (Sarkar et al., 2018; Zhai et al., 2012). The evidence of functional groups on the surface of AuNPs was confirmed by FTIR spectrum (Supplementary data; Fig. S1 (A)). The characteristic absorption band of 3390 cm−1 denoted OH stretching. The IR peak at 1392 and 1596 cm−1 indicating the existence of the COO− group on the surface of AuNPs (Shi et al., 2014). The presence of carboxylic groups make the AuNPs negatively charged, which was confirmed by the zeta potential (ζ = −30 mV Fig. 2(C)).

The positively charged (+3.88 mV) CQDs and negatively charged (−30 mV) AuNPs at pH 7.0 were confirmed through the zeta potential analyzer as shown in Fig. 2(C). Due to opposition in charge CQDs electrostatically interacted with AuNPs resulted in the cluster formation of CQDs-AuNPs as observed from TEM image (Fig. 1D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of AuNPs on fluorescence intensity of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As presented in Fig. 2(A) the absorption spectrum of AuNPs satisfactorily overlapped with the fluorescence excitation and emission spectrum of CQDs so that the IFE of AuNPs quenched the FL intensity of CQDs. Moreover, the FL intensity of CQDs progressively decreased along with the various concentrations of AuNPs ranging from 0.0 to 8.1 nm as shown in Fig. 3 (A). The AuNPs was highly dispersed and randomly scattered in aqueous solution due to the electrostatic repulsion between negative citrate ions which acts against the van der Waals attraction between AuNPs (Liu, Luais, & Gooding, 2011; Zhang et al., 2012) with SPR absorption peak at 524 nm and wine red in color. For the confirmation of IFE fluorescence, lifetime analysis of CQDs in the presence of AuNPs and ALD was investigated. The fluorescence lifetime measurement was obtained as 15.43 ns, 15.29 ns and 15.09 ns for CQDs (a), CQDs-AuNPs (b) and CQDs-AuNPs-ALD (d), respectively, which was hardly changed with AuNPs and ALD as shown in Fig. 2(D). These results further confirmed that the quenched FL intensity of CQDs by the AuNPs was mainly recognized to the IFE of AuNPs.

As soon as the positively charged (+3.88 mV) CQDs and negatively charged (−30.7 mV) AuNPs come closer, they get agglomerated due to the electrostatic interaction between them. The FL intensity of CQDs diminishes with the increase in concentrations of AuNPs. The FL intensity quenched by the IFE of AuNPs can be calculated using the following formula
(1)
F
Q u e n c h e d
=
F 0
- F
F 0
where, F and F0 are the FL intensity of CQDs in the presence and absence of AuNPs, respectively. It clearly showed in the Fig. 3(A) that the FL intensity of CQDs approximately 75% quenched along with the increasing concentration of AuNPs up to 8.1 nM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhancement of CQDs fluorescence due to the intercalation effect of ALD
ce:section.ce:para: In the developed system the optical applicability of CQDs-AuNPs probe was experimentally tested against the effect of ALD on developed probe. Fig. 3(C) shows the fluorescence spectra of (a) CQDs, (b) CQDs-ALD, (c) CQDs-AuNPs-ALD and (d) AuNPs and under same experimental condition. The fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs exhibit intensive peak at 458 nm when excited at a wavelength of 350 nm as shown in Fig. 3(C). In the presence of the ALD, the fluorescence spectrum of CQDs does not show any significant change in the intensity as well as peak position results indicates that there is no interaction occurred between CQDs and ALD.

The presence of CQDs can induce the aggregation of AuNPs and hence the intensity of CQDs got significantly quenched due to IFE of AuNPs. However, with the addition of ALD into AuNPs, there was no aggregation indicating that ALD plays role of anti-aggregation agents which prevented the agglomeration of AuNPs. UV–Vis spectra of AuNPs (a) along with CQDs (b) and ALD (c) (Fig. 3D) indicated that, the AuNPs gets agglomerated with addition of CQDs and a new absorbance peak appeared at 620 nm. The similar results are shown in TEM images (Fig. 1C). ALD acts as an intercalated layer between CQDs and AuNPs which acts an anti-aggregation agent and prevents the aggregations of AuNPs, which was clearly shown in UV–Vis spectrum (Fig. 3 D) and TEM image as well in Fig. 1(D). As a result of this, the IFE of AuNPs on FL intensity of CQDs reduced and recovered the FL intensity of CQDs. Therefore, it is contemplated that the turn on fluorescence method can be proposed for the detection of ALD.

Scheme 1 shows the systematic fluorometric detection of ALD based on anti-aggregation of AuNPs. In the developed system, CQDs and ALD acts as aggregation and anti-aggregation reagents for AuNPs, respectively. Upon the addition of CQDs into the AuNPs solution, due to the ligand exchange between amine rich CQDs and negatively charged citrate ions of AuNPs induce the aggregation of AuNPs. As a result of this the AuNPs color change from wine red to blue and quenching the FL intensity of CQDs through IFE of AuNPs (Scheme 1A).

It is well known that the electron rich nitrogen (N) atoms are easy to bind onto the electron lacking surface of metal NPs through the coordination interaction (Leng et al., 2013). In fact the N atom has strong binding affinity to AuNPs, and the Au-N bond energy is analogous to that of the Au-S bond (Huang et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2011).

Fig. 4(C) shows the interferent study. It was observed that the compounds with N or both N and S group exhibit the slightest effect on this system. The observable fact further proves that when the ALD was introduced into AuNPs, ALD attached onto the surface of AuNPs previous to CQDs and lead to the anti-aggregation of the AuNPs. As a result of these experiments, it was considered that ALD molecule rather than CQDs get engaged on the surface of AuNPs through Au-S and Au-N bond configuration. Thus, with increasing concentrations of ALD, most of the ALD molecules were getting absorbed on the surface of the AuNPs and likely most of the CQDs molecules were free in the solution and hence increased the FL intensity of CQDs as well as the color of the AuNPs kept wine red again as shown in Scheme1B.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of experimental conditions for detection of ALD.
ce:section.ce:para:
For the detection of ALD the experimental parameters were optimized for the recovery of fluorescent intensity of CQDs-AuNPs probe after removing the IFE of AuNPs. The pH of the solution, AuNPs concentration and incubation time for the recovery of FL intensity of CQDs in the presence of ALD were investigated (Fig. S2). The fluorescence recovery efficiency of the system in the presence of ALD was calculated through the following equation:
(2)
R e c o v e r y
e f f i c i e n c y =
F -
F 0
F 0
where, F and F0 denoted the FL intensity of CQDs-AuNPs probe with and without the presence of ALD.

Fig. S2 (A) shows the pH effect on the fluorescence recovery. It clearly indicated that the fluorescence recovery increased from pH 3.5 to pH 7.0 and then dropped dramatically from pH 7.7 to pH 8.5 due to the protonation - deprotonation of amino and carboxyl group on the CQDs surface (Song et al., 2016; Wang, Chen, Li, & Wu, 2019). Besides this, AuNPs concentrations also have the influence over the fluorescence recovery of CQDs as shown in Fig. S2 (B). The fluorescence recovery of CQDs gradually increased up to certain concentration and then decreased along with the increased concentrations of AuNPs from 0.46 nM to 8.1 nM and maximum recovery obtained with 4.24 nM AuNPs concentration. This is probably due to the increasing number of AuNPs in the solution compared to ALD molecules which adsorbed on the surface of AuNPs and hence, again quenched the FL intensity of CQDs.

Fig. S2 (C) shows that the FL intensity recovery of CQDs increases with incubation time between ALD and the CQDs-AuNPs system and the graphs revealed that the maximum fluorescence recovery was obtained within 45 min of incubation time and remains same for another 5 min and then again decreased (inset of Fig. S2 (C)). Hence, the optimized parameters for the detection of ALD were as; AuNPs concentration of 4.24 nM; the pH of the solution of 7.0 and the incubation time of 45 min for the detection of ALD. These optimized values were kept for all the further measurements.

Under the optimized experimental conditions, the FL intensity spectra of CQDs-AuNPs probe along with different concentrations of ALD is shown in Fig. 4 (A). Results clearly indicate that, the FL intensity of CQDs was increased progressively due to the removal of IFE of AuNPs in the presence of increasing concentration of ALD. The fluorescence recovery efficiency of CQDs-AuNPs probes as shown in Fig. 4 (B) was calculated using Eq. (2) along with different concentrations of ALD between 0 and 76 µg L−1. It was obvious that the presence of different concentration of ALD significantly reduced the IFE of AuNPs and hence, increased the FL intensity of CQDs. The inset of Fig. 4 (B) clearly indicates that the fluorescence recovery efficiency shows a linear response with ALD concentration within a range of 3.8–76 µg L−1 with the lower detection limit 3.02 µg L−1.

A comparison between the proposed work and other reported methods for the quantitative detection of ALD were summarized in Table 1 . It can be seen that the dynamic range and LOD of this method has some improvement in comparison with some other reported methods. Therefore, according to the above mentioned results, the CQDs-AuNPs probe as a novel system that may fulfill the requirement of simple, sensitive and selective detection of ALD.

To validate the selectivity of proposed developed probe, the FL intensity of CQDs-AuNPs probe was tested against different common existing interfering substances in fruits and vegetables samples. Fig. 4(C) clearly shows that the maximum recovery of FL intensity of CQDs-AuNPs probe was obtained with ALD. In this experiment, an optimized amount of interfering species were separately added in the AuNPs as done with ALD and the recovery in FL intensity were recorded. Thus, from the above experiments, it was concluded that the CQDs-AuNPs probe was highly selective towards ALD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of ALD in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of the developed sensor was checked in the real sample as well as in spiked samples by recording the FL intensity of CQDs-AuNPs probe in the presence of these samples. For that, various food samples such as cucumber, tomato, potato, oranges, grapes, and pomegranate extracted juices with soft drinks like Pepsi, Coca-Cola and Thumps-Up were tested to monitor the presence of ALD. It was observed that there were no traces of ALD detected in the real samples (Supplementary data S3). Then, the real samples were spiked with the known concentration of 200 nM (38 µg L−1) for ALD and recorded the FL intensity (Supplementary data S4). The comparative histogram plot of real and spiked samples are shown in Fig. 4(D).

The spiked samples were prepared by direct exposure of ALD to cucumber and tomato with an unknown concentration of ALD. The FL intensity of CQDs-AuNPs probe was recorded in the presence of extracted solution and calculated the unknown concentration of ALD. The system recovery confirmed the traces of ALD in spiked sample and the concentration was obtained as 1.90 µg L−1, and 0.94 µg L−1 for cucumber and tomato, respectively, with the help of the calibration plot of the developed system. To validate the results, the LC-MS (Zong, Qu, & Lu, 2013) study was performed with samples having an unknown concentration of ALD and obtained a concentration 1.17 µg L−1 for cucumber and no traces were obtained for tomato as shown in Fig. 4E . The concentration obtained through the developed system was very small and there were no traces obtained in LC-MS in tomato extraction. This proves that the developed system is more sensitive as compared to the standard analytical method, LC-MS. Therefore, the developed sensor can be used in real sample detection also.

In this present work, CQDs-AuNPs probe was developed for the detection of ALD which have high selectivity and sensitivity for food samples. Upon addition of CQDs in AuNPs, the positively charged amine rich CQDs interact with negatively charged citrate ions of AuNPs induce the aggregation of AuNPs. As a result of this the FL intensity of CQDs progressively quenched along with rising concentration of AuNPs, through the IFE of AuNPs. Upon addition of ALD, due to the intercalation of ALD which plays the role of anti-aggregations agent and the IFE demines and the FL intensity of CQDs recovered along with increasing concentration of ALD. The FL intensity of CQDs increased due to the interaction of ALD with AuNPs through Au-S and Au-N bond configuration, which prevented aggregation of AuNPs and hence reduced its IFE. Under the optimum experimental condition ALD sensitivity may be detected with a lower detection limit 3.02 µg L−1. The performance of the system was checked in the spiked samples of fruits and vegetables, and further validation of the proposed system was done with the existing analytical LC-MS technique and found that the system has excellent sensitivity for ALD. It is expected that, the CQDs-AuNPs probe developed for the detection of ALD will have enormous potential for application in environmental monitoring and food safety.
Reena K. Sajwan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing - original draft. G.B.V.S. Lakshmi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Pratima R. Solanki: .

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: One of the authors is thankful to Indo-Russia for financial support from. Authors are also thankful to AIRF, JNU, for their characterization facilities. This work was supported by Department of Biotechnology (Indo-Russia (DBT/IC-2/Indo-Russia/2017-19/02 and DST Purse. GBVS Lakshmi is grateful to DST for funding through the DST Women Scientist Project

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2020.127835.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
